data.title: Nasdaq jumps over 2% on Nvidia results and strong Sept jobs data
data.description:
U.S. stocks jumped up on Thursday after Nvidia’s (NVDA) results reset the market’s risk tone for the time being. Adding to the momentum, the long-awaited September jobs report finally landed, delivering payroll growth that easily topped expectations.

Leading the charge higher on the session was the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) as it was last up by 2.2%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) had pushed up by 1.6% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) climbed by 1.4%. 

From a sector-by-sector point of view, all of the 11 S&P segments find themselves up in positive territory and are led by Communication Services. Going in the other direction, Health Care has been the weakest performer thus far.

As for stocks that were on the move, Nvidia was +3.2% after adding more than $200B to its market cap with its post-earnings rally, nearing the $200/share level it lost early this month.

Thursday's spotlight was on the September nonfarm payrolls report, which was initially supposed to come out on October 3. However, the longest U.S. government shutdown in history derailed that release.

The data showed the U.S. labor market added 119K jobs in September, easily outstripping the consensus of 50K. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4% in the month, a tad higher than the expected 4.3%.

“Thursday's jobs report was much stronger than expected and it's possible that the Federal Reserve may take more of a wait and see approach to rates in December, especially since there is still uncertainty over the economic data, which has only first started to come back online after the drought from the government shutdown. Thursday's jobs report suggests that the labor market is not as weak as feared,” Alexander Guiliano, chief investment officer, Resonate Wealth Partners stated.

Moreover, Treasury yields shifted lower as the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved down by 2 basis points to 4.11% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) pushed down by 2 basis points to 3.57%.

data.title: BigBear, Easy Lease and Vigilix sign MOU to advance Pahang Aerospace city development
data.description:
• BigBear (BBAI) announced on Thursday a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with Pahang Aerospace City Development Berhad and its established partners Easy Lease and Vigilix Technology at the Dubai Air Show.
  • The success of this project is expected to attract further significant domestic and foreign investment. This in turn will strengthen Malaysia’s aerospace and support industries, enabling it to establish the region’s first international spaceport. 
  • This collaboration will support the company for innovation and growth in Southeast Asia.
  • BBAI shares up 6% at $6.20.

data.title: Atkore outlines FY26 mid-single-digit volume growth and expands strategic alternatives review
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Atkore Inc. (ATKR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO William Waltz announced an expansion of strategic alternatives, stating "the Board has now decided to expand the scope of the strategic alternatives to include a potential sale or merger of the whole company." He also confirmed his intent to remain as CEO through the conclusion of the review and discussed ongoing divestitures, including the potential sale of the HDPE business and two other modest noncore assets anticipated for divestment in late Q1 2026 or early Q2. Waltz added, "we plan to cease manufacturing operations at the 3 manufacturing facilities previously announced in the second quarter of fiscal 2026."
  • Waltz highlighted, "Organic volume was up 1.4% in the fourth quarter with contributions from both segments. Notably, we saw double-digit growth in our plastic pipe, conduit and fittings product category."
  • CFO John Deitzer reported, "Net sales in the fourth quarter were $752 million, and our adjusted EPS was $0.69. Adjusted EBITDA for the fourth quarter was $71 million. We generated a net loss of $54 million in the fourth quarter." Deitzer also detailed a $19 million noncash goodwill impairment charge related to the mechanical tube business and a $67 million impairment charge related to certain HDPE assets.
  • COO John Pregenzer described the breadth of the product portfolio and noted, "Demand for electricity continues to increase. The need for power centers around the expansion of data centers to support AI."

Outlook

  • For FY26, Deitzer stated, "we anticipate a mid-single-digit volume growth in FY '26, driven by expected growth in all 5 of our product areas. For the first quarter of FY '26, we are expecting net sales in the range of $645 million to $655 million and adjusted EBITDA between $55 million and $65 million. We expect adjusted EPS to be in the range of $0.55 and $0.75. For the full year, we expect FY '26 net sales in the range of $3.0 billion to $3.1 billion and adjusted EBITDA between $340 million and $360 million. Adjusted EPS is expected to be in the range of $5.05 and $5.55."
  • Management announced the cessation of quarterly outlooks starting in calendar year 2026, stating, "we have made the decision not to provide a quarterly outlook starting in calendar year 2026 with our fiscal first quarter earnings call."

Financial Results

  • Net sales for Q4 were $752 million, with adjusted EPS of $0.69 and adjusted EBITDA of $71 million, impacted by $6 million of onetime inventory adjustments and $5 million in nonroutine items. Excluding these, adjusted EBITDA would have been $82 million.
  • The company reported returning $144 million to shareholders through share repurchases and dividends during the year. For the full year, net sales were $2.9 billion and adjusted EPS was $6.05 with adjusted EBITDA of $386 million.
  • Segmentally, Electrical net sales were $519 million, with organic volume growth, while the Safety and Infrastructure (S&I) segment saw a 4% net sales increase and improved EBITDA margins due to productivity improvements.
  • The company highlighted three consecutive years of organic volume growth and expects volume growth in FY26 to be mid-single digits.

Q&A

  • Justin Clare, ROTH Capital Partners: Asked about the implied pricing benefit in FY26 guidance. Deitzer answered, "we've seen sequential price increases in our steel conduit business. There are some other businesses where we've had pricing growth as well... there would be some embedded benefit -- or increase, I should say, at the ASP line." Clare further inquired about margin improvement, with Deitzer explaining, "we do have line of sight to a lot of the construction services and the mega projects in Q2 to Q4."
  • David Tarantino, KeyBanc: Questioned the range of outcomes for the strategic review and magnitude of divestments. Waltz replied, "it's early on... the process... can be the full range from... selling the whole co to... the Board decides that the best thing is to continue to run it as is."
  • Tarantino also asked about cost savings. Pregenzer stated, "we would expect to see about $10 million to $12 million in cost reductions across the fiscal year."
  • Andrew Kaplowitz, Citigroup: Asked about the $50 million headwind for '26 and market imports. Deitzer confirmed, "continued price versus cost headwinds going in '26 versus '25," with expectations for mitigation through volume and productivity.
  • Chris Moore, CJS Securities: Sought details on plant closures and HDPE divestment. Waltz explained plant closures and noted HDPE market growth with, "volumes are coming back."
  • Deane Dray, RBC: Queried about running the business for cash and dividend suspension. Waltz responded, "no, we have no discussion in the Board meeting on suspending dividends."
  • Chris Dankert, Loop Capital: Asked about the back-half-weighted guidance. Deitzer pointed to seasonality and investments in solar and mega projects as drivers for stronger performance in the second half of the year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive yet probing tone, focusing on strategic alternatives, guidance clarity, headwinds, and cost actions. Questions probed financial impacts and strategic rationale.
  • Management sounded confident in prepared remarks, with Waltz emphasizing "conviction on our teams and are focused on delivering to our plan." In Q&A, responses were detailed but occasionally hedged with qualifiers such as "it's early on" and "we'll see how that plays out."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone shifted from transition and succession planning to actively addressing strategic alternatives and operational restructuring. Analyst skepticism increased slightly, particularly regarding the expanded review, cost controls, and headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a significant shift with the announcement of an expanded strategic review, including the potential sale or merger of the entire company—this was not discussed last quarter.
  • Management's focus moved from succession planning and productivity improvements to maximizing shareholder value through asset divestitures and operational optimization.
  • Guidance for FY26 is specific, with mid-single-digit volume growth and explicit targets for net sales, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted EPS, whereas the previous quarter provided only directional commentary.
  • Strategic emphasis on data centers, solar, and electrification remains, but the current quarter highlighted the impacts of impairment charges and cost reduction initiatives from facility closures.
  • Analyst questions this quarter were more focused on the implications of strategic alternatives and cost-saving measures rather than pure operational performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing price versus cost headwinds, particularly in the first half of FY26, and noted exposure to market volatility and commodity fluctuations.
  • Facility closures and asset impairments were highlighted as immediate challenges, with mitigation through operational consolidation and divestitures.
  • Analyst concerns centered on the magnitude and timeline of cost savings, potential for further divestitures, and clarity on strategic outcomes.

Final Takeaway

Atkore's management is executing on a broadened set of strategic alternatives, with the Board now considering a potential sale or merger of the entire company, alongside the ongoing divestiture of noncore assets and closure of selected manufacturing facilities. Guidance for FY26 projects mid-single-digit volume growth and a return to year-over-year adjusted EBITDA growth in FY27, while operational focus remains on electrical infrastructure, end-market expansion, and productivity gains. Management asserts confidence in delivering shareholder value amid a complex environment of pricing headwinds, cost actions, and market opportunities, reinforced by strong cash flow and a resilient product portfolio.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tether backs LatAm crypto firm Parfin to expand institutional USDT adoption
data.description:
Tether, the digital asset company behind the USDT (USDT-USD) stablecoin, has invested in Latin American crypto platform Parfin to accelerate the institutional adoption of USDT across the region, it said on Thursday.

The investment, terms of which were not disclosed, reflects Tether's push to support financial institutions in using dollar-pegged USDT (USDT-USD) -- the world's largest stablecoin by market cap -- as a core settlement asset for use cases like global transactions, real-world asset tokenization and yield-bearing credit markets.

Parfin provides tools for digital asset custody, tokenization, trading and management in LatAm, which has recorded nearly $1.5T in crypto transaction volume, Tether noted, citing a recent Chainalysis report.

data.title: U.S. growth is set to reaccelerate in 2026, Apollo’s Slok notes
data.description:
Apollo chief economist Torsten Slok says the U.S. economy is positioned for a meaningful pickup in 2026, marking a shift from the more cautious outlook that prevailed earlier this year.

When the trade war began in March, economists broadly trimmed their growth expectations, reflecting heightened uncertainty around global supply chains and investment decisions. 

Those projections have steadily improved in recent months. A series of trade agreements reached over the summer and fall helped ease tensions, prompting analysts to reassess the economic outlook. As policy clarity increased, forecasts for medium-term growth began to move higher. 

Slok now sees several forces converging to support a stronger expansion by 2026. A further reduction in trade-related uncertainty and a modestly weaker U.S. dollar are expected to lift exports and investment. In addition, the recently enacted One Big Beautiful Bill is set to become a major catalyst.

Slok noted that according to estimates from the Congressional Budget Office, the legislation could add nearly one percentage point to GDP growth in 2026, largely through provisions that encourage accelerated depreciation. 

Together, Apollo believes these factors suggest the economy may regain momentum after a period of uneven performance, positioning 2026 as a potential year of renewed acceleration.

Market Tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: Nvidia's home run results provide uplift across spectrum of AI infrastructure names
data.description:
Nvidia's (NVDA) home run third-quarter results and guidance provided an uplift for an array of stocks related to artificial intelligence infrastructure.

Server and storage company Supermicro (SMCI) edged up nearly 3% during early market action on Thursday. Fellow server solutions companies Dell Technologies (DELL) and HP Enterprise (HPE) were up 4% and 1.5%, respectively.

Earlier this week, Dell and Nvidia announced a new partnership to integrate advanced Nvidia hardware into its servers to provide enterprises with the power to drive AI applications and high-performance computing. Similarly, Supermicro revealed its new AI factory cluster, which it said is a full-stack solution comprised of Supermicro's portfolio of AI-optimized systems, Nvidia software stack and Nvidia Spectrum-X Ethernet.

Cisco Systems (CSCO) had inched up 1.5%. Last week, during Cisco's earnings call, CEO Charles Robbins highlighted an expansion of its partnership with Nvidia and the new N9100 switch based on Spectrum-X silicon.

"We are now the first Nvidia partner to offer networking compliant with their cloud reference architecture," Robbins said. "The N9100 available in the second half of fiscal year '26 will provide the operational consistency and flexibility needed for sovereign and neocloud providers to build and manage AI at scale."

Fellow chipmaker Intel (INTC) had climbed 3%. Intel is working with Nvidia to jointly develop multiple generations of custom data center and PC products that accelerate applications and workloads across hyperscale, enterprise and consumer markets.

GE Vernova (GEV), which provides power equipment for Nvidia, had increased 4.5%. 

Cadence Design Systems (CDNS), who provides software and agentic AI solutions for Nvidia, was up nearly 3%.

Optical component suppliers Coherent (COHR), Fabrinet (FN) and Lumentum (LITE) had climbed 8%, 4% and 3.6%, respectively.

Celestica (CLS), which is a partner and competitor with Nvidia for backend scale-out AI infrastructure, was up 3%.

Nvidia chip rival AMD (AMD) had inched up 2% during early market action. And Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), which manufactures Nvidia's GPUs, had increased 3%.  

Semiconductor equipment manufacturers were all trading higher as well. With ASML (ASML), Lam Research (LRCX) and KLA (KLAC) up 1%, 3% and 2.6%, respectively.

Cloud service providers, which run the AI data centers fueled by Nvidia GPUs, were also rising. CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS) were up 7% and 5.5%, respectively. Oracle (ORCL) has increased 1.9%.

data.title: Uber, Starship Technologies to bring autonomous delivery to Europe
data.description:
Uber (UBER) and Starship Technologies are partnering to bring autonomous sidewalk delivery robots for last-mile delivery with the first deployment expected in the UK by December and in multiple European countries next year.

Starship’s AI-powered robots have completed over 9 million deliveries across seven countries, enabling Uber Eats (UBER) to offer next-generation delivery to its European customers. The robots will operate at Level 4 autonomy (without human intervention) and can complete deliveries in less than 30 minutes for distances up to 2 miles.

U.S. deployment of Starship robots is planned for 2027.

“Autonomous delivery is an exciting part of how we see the future of Uber Eats,” said Sarfraz Maredia, Uber’s global head of autonomous, adding that the partnership with Starship enables Uber to bring this capability across multiple continents by capitalizing on Starship’s large autonomous delivery network.

Over the past 11 years, Starship’s fleet of 2,700 robots have operated in more than 100 cities across 15 countries.

The partnership with Starship builds on Uber’s (UBER) efforts to expand autonomous delivery through collaborations. Within the U.S., the company uses robots for deliveries in Austin, Chicago, Dallas-Fort Worth, Miami, Los Angeles, Atlanta, and Jersey City through partnerships with Serve Robotics and Avride.

data.title: HSBC revamps trading unit to boost debt-financing operations - report
data.description:
HSBC Holdings (HSBC) is overhauling its trading division as part of CEO Georges Elhedery's campaign to transform it into a debt-financing leader, according to a media report on Thursday.

The company's Group-of-10 rates trading unit will merge with its foreign exchange, emerging markets rates, and commodities desks to create a global macro division, Bloomberg News reported, citing a memo to HSBC staff.  At the same time, derivatives clearing services will become part of the global equities team.

The rest of HSBC's (HSBC) global debt markets businesses — encompassing high yield and investment-grade trading to emerging market credit businesses — will operate under the global credit and financing group. The resulting group will work closely with the bank's investment bankers and underwriters, the memo said.

The restructuring comes after HSBC (HSBC) shut most of its advisory and equity underwriting operations in the U.S., U.K., and continental Europe, in order to focus on debt capital markets in regions where the bank has the heft to compete with Wall Street giants.

The latest moves highlight “our ambition to build a financing powerhouse,” Patrick George, HSBC’s global head of markets and securities services, said in the memo.

Volkan Benihasim will head the new global macro team, while Franck Lacour will continue to run the equities division, Bloomberg said, citing the memo. The bank expects to run a short, internal selection process for naming a new head of global credit and financing.

Mehmet Mazi, HSBC's head of global debt markets, will explore other opportunities, the article said, citing people familiar with the matter.

HSBC (HSBC) stock edged up 0.1% in Thursday morning trading.

data.title: Starbucks sees little store disruption even as barista strike broadens
data.description:
Starbucks (SBUX) faces an escalating strike of baristas in the U.S. in what has become the largest union pushback against the Seattle-based company in its history. 

The union announced on Thursday it will now be striking at 95 stores in 65 cities, with some 2,000 baristas now engaged in the action. On Wednesday, baristas and allies also picketed and held a rally outside the company’s distribution center in York, Pennsylvania. "Our union isn’t slowing down... in fact, we’re just getting started," read a social media post from Starbucks Workers United. 

For its part, Starbucks (SBUX) noted that there are no disruptions to its operations in York and highlighted that this year's Red Cup Day was the strongest in the company's history in terms of sales and store traffic. "As we’ve said, 99% of our 17,000 U.S. locations remain open and welcoming customers, including many the union publicly stated would strike but never closed or have since reopened," updated Starbucks spokeswoman Jaci Anderson.

Earlier in the month, Starbucks (SBUX) said it was disappointed that Workers United, who only represent around 4% of its partners, voted to authorize a strike instead of returning to the bargaining table. Starbucks (SBUX) described the barista position as one of the best jobs in retail, with average pay of more than $19 an hour and over $30 an hour when including benefits.

Shares of Starbucks (SBUX) were up 1.7% in Thursday morning trading but have traded mainly sideways over the last six months.

data.title: GSK inks deal with LTZ Therapeutics to develop hematologic cancer therapies
data.description:
• GSK (GSK) has signed a deal with LTZ Therapeutics to use the latter immune-engager platform to develop first-in-class myeloid cell engager (MCE) treatments for hematologic cancers and solid tumors.
  • Terms call for LTZ to receive a $50M upfront payment, followed by the potential for milestone payments as well as tiered royalties on any products developed through the partnership.
  • GSK can develop up to four MCE therapies.
  • LTZ, which has operations in California's Bay Area as well as Shenzhen, China, noted that "MCEs are an emerging class of immuno-oncology treatments designed to use the body’s own immune system to recognize and kill tumor cells, providing a novel approach to target cancers with a favorable safety profile."
